news.title: How Luis de la Fuente transformed Spain into football's most complete team and masterminded a glorious World Cup triumph | Football News
news.lede: At first glance, Luis de la Fuente could easily be mistaken for a college professor — calm, measured and scholarly, hardly the sort of figure who intimidates his pupils. Yet the 65-year-old grew up in an era when Spanish football was decided by who survived the tackle, not who passed around it. As Athletic Club's left-back, he made 196 appearances across the 1980s and early 1990s and was on the pitch the day Andoni Goikoetxea shattered Diego Maradona's ankle in the infamous "Butcher of Bilbao" match.Four decades later, De la Fuente built Spain's calmest and most complete side. They conceded just one goal throughout the World Cup, the fewest ever by a men's champion, and reclaimed football's biggest prize by playing with remarkable clarity rather than chaos.

news.title: Leandro Paredes turns the page quickly, returns to Boca Juniors training days after World Cup final
news.lede: For many players, reaching a World Cup final is the culmination of a career and often comes with a well-earned break afterward. Just days after helping Argentina reach the 2026 World Cup Final, where La Albiceleste fell 1-0 to Spain in extra time, the veteran midfielder was already back on the training ground with Boca Juniors, wasting no time as he shifts his focus to the second half of the club season. Paredes' quick return sends a clear message: the disappointment of missing out on another world title won't keep him away from Boca's next challenge. After returning to Argentina with the rest of the national team delegation, Boca's captain headed straight to the club's training complex in Ezeiza to reunite with head coach Rodolfo Arruabarrena and his teammates.

news.title: Missing the World Cup? Relief is on the way next July with Women’s World Cup
news.lede: It is the women’s turn now. The next football World Cup, the women’s edition, is set for the next northern hemisphere summer in Brazil. Just as Spain won the men’s trophy in the recently concluded tournament, that nation’s women were the champions three years ago. Currently the top-ranked women’s team in the world, Spain topped England 1-0 in the 2023 Women’s World Cup final in Sydney.

news.title: George Kittle, other NFL players push for grass after World Cup resurfacing
news.lede: The World Cup is finished, but NFL players hope grass fields are not. After football venues including MetLife Stadium in New Jersey swapped out synthetic turf for natural grass for the global soccer tournament, big-name players including George Kittle, Breece Hall and Jonathan Taylor urged the NFL to stick with grass. “Grass looks great at MetLife! Can we keep it that way @NFL??

news.title: The players who won hearts at the 2026 World Cup
news.lede: The 2026 World Cup may be over, but Spain's victory wasn't the only thing fans were talking about. As matches unfolded across Mexico, the United States, and Canada, the tournament delivered unforgettable moments both on and off the pitch. It wasn't just about spectacular goals, tactical masterclasses, and dramatic knockout clashes. A standout group of players also captured attention with their effortless style, charisma, and undeniable star power.Among the fan favorites were Jude Bellingham, known for his polished, youthful elegance; Antoine Griezmann, who brought classic French sophistication and a killer smile; and Rodrigo De Paul, who won over crowds with his signature rebel-chic look.

news.cat: ABC NEWS & HEADLINES – AUSTRALIAN BROADCASTING CORPORATION
news.title: Spanish chief accuses FIFA boss of 'destroying the football industry'
news.lede: FIFA president Gianni Infantino (left) has come under heavy fire from Spain's La Liga president Javier Tebas. (Getty Images: Chris Brunskill). La Liga boss Javier Tebas has called for the resignation of FIFA president Gianni Infantino. Tebas says Infantino is "destroying the football industry" by expanding international competitions such as the World Cup.

news.title: World Cup becoming a 64-team tournament would create logistical challenges - so can Gianni Infantino's idea work?
news.lede: This year's World Cup is finally over - and after 48 teams took part, it was a long, long tournament. But just as we bid to say goodbye to the 2026 edition, this competition may be getting even bigger. FIFA president Gianni Infantino says any further expansion of the World Cup for 2030 - where six countries are set to host the tournament - ‌will be discussed now the 2026 World Cup has finished. And one solution FIFA are discussing is a 64-team tournament.

news.title: What's next for World Cup hero Ferran Torres? FC Barcelona faces fight to keep Spain's match-winner
news.lede: One goal can change everything. Just days after scoring the extra-time winner that gave Spain a 1-0 victory over Argentina in the 2026 World Cup Final, Ferran Torres has become one of the hottest names on the European transfer market. The striker's stock has never been higher, and while FC Barcelona is determined to keep him, interest from some of Europe's biggest clubs is threatening to complicate the situation. At the front of that list is Paris Saint-Germain, which is reportedly pushing hard to bring the Spanish international to France ahead of the 2026-27 season.
